title: Where Money Comes From
summary: Why we use money at all, and why things cost what they cost.
doThisToday: Look at one price tag today and guess why it's priced that way — lots of demand? Expensive to make? Little competition?

cards.prompt: Before money existed, people traded stuff directly for other stuff.
cards.explanation: That's called bartering. Problem: if you have chickens and need shoes, you have to find a shoemaker who happens to want chickens right now. Money fixes that — it's a tool everyone agrees to accept, so you can trade with anyone.
cards.digDeeper: Economists call this the "double coincidence of wants" problem — bartering only works when two people each happen to have exactly what the other wants, at the same time. Good money solves it by having three properties: it's a medium of exchange (everyone accepts it), a store of value (it doesn't spoil or vanish), and a unit of account (it lets you compare the price of totally different things, like chickens and shoes, on the same scale).

cards.prompt: Put the history of money in the order it actually happened.
cards.explanation: Each step solved a problem with the one before it — commodities were easier to trade than random goods, coins were more durable and portable than commodities, paper was lighter than metal, and digital money is instant.

cards.prompt: Salt used to be seriously valuable money.
cards.frontText: What everyday word comes from Roman soldiers sometimes being paid in salt?
cards.backText: "Salary." It comes from the Latin "salarium," tied to salt payments — salt was that valuable before refrigeration existed, since it was one of the only ways to preserve food.
cards.explanation: Money has taken a lot of forms throughout history — the idea is old, the format keeps changing.

cards.prompt: You mow lawns for cash instead of trading lawn-mowing directly for a used bike. Why is that better?
options: It's not actually better, cash is just tradition
You can save the cash and use it for anything later, not just a bike
Cash weighs less than a bike
cards.explanation: Money is flexible. Cash from mowing lawns can become a bike, concert tickets, or savings — whatever you decide later. That flexibility is the entire point of money.

cards.prompt: So why does a PS5 cost more than a phone case? Supply and demand.
cards.explanation: Demand = how many people want something. Supply = how much of it exists. When lots of people want something and there isn't much of it, the price goes up. When there's tons of it and few people want it, the price drops.
cards.digDeeper: This is also why the same item can have wildly different prices depending on timing and location — bottled water costs more at a stadium than a grocery store because supply is fixed (the vendor controls it) and demand is captive (you can't easily leave to find a cheaper one). Companies aren't being sneaky when they do this; they're reading the same supply-and-demand curve you're learning right now.

cards.prompt: A new gaming console just launched and stores keep selling out immediately. What happens to resale prices online?
options: They go up — high demand, low supply
They go down — everyone already has one
They stay exactly at the store price
cards.explanation: Limited supply plus high demand pushes prices up, which is exactly why scalpers exist — they're betting on that gap.

cards.prompt: True or false: the price of something is just whatever it costs the company to make it.
cards.explanation: Cost to make is only part of it. Companies price based on what people are willing to pay, how much competition exists, and how badly people want it — not just materials and labor.

cards.prompt: Money itself doesn't create value — it just makes trading value way easier.
cards.explanation: Everything you learn from here on out is really about one question: how do you get, keep, and grow the value that money represents?
